stem: According to Wairangi (2011), mental health nurses need knowledge of counselling and therapy techniques. Which statement MOST precisely identifies ALL the skills and knowledge domains listed — without adding or omitting any?
options: Excellent nursing skills, knowledge of psychiatric disorders, knowledge of different diseases and illnesses, knowledge of medicines and treatments, knowledge of counselling and therapy techniques, an understanding of the Mental Health Act, oral and written communication skills, and good listening skills

Excellent nursing skills, knowledge of psychiatric disorders, knowledge of diseases and illnesses, knowledge of medicines, knowledge of therapy techniques, and an understanding of the Patient Rights Act

Excellent nursing skills, knowledge of psychiatric disorders, knowledge of diseases and illnesses, knowledge of medicines and treatments, oral and written communication skills, good listening skills, and organisational and decision-making skills — excluding the Mental Health Act

Excellent nursing skills, knowledge of nursing theories, knowledge of psychiatric disorders, knowledge of therapy techniques, understanding of the Mental Health Act, good listening skills, and interpersonal communication skills

stem: The therapeutic use of self is defined in the notes. Which statement MOST completely and precisely captures its full definition?
options: The ability to use one's personality consciously and in full awareness to establish relatedness and to structure nursing intervention, requiring self-insight, self-understanding, and understanding of human behaviour dynamics; the ability to interpret one's own and others' behaviour; and the ability to intervene effectively in nursing situations

The ability to use one's clinical knowledge consciously and in full awareness to establish therapeutic relationships, requiring knowledge of psychiatric disorders, understanding of human behaviour dynamics, and the ability to intervene effectively through medication management

The ability to use one's personality consciously to establish relatedness, requiring self-insight and understanding of human behaviour, with a primary focus on group therapy facilitation and psychoeducation for patients and their families

The ability to use one's professional skills and knowledge consciously to structure nursing interventions, requiring self-understanding and understanding of the nurse-patient relationship dynamics within the therapeutic milieu

stem: CASE STUDY: A registered psychiatric mental health nurse (RPMN) is described as working in multiple settings. A student lists the settings but includes one that is NOT mentioned in the notes. Which setting is NOT listed as a setting where the RPMN may work?
options: The patient's own home and a community health care centre
A secure residential unit and a psychiatric hospital
A general hospital outpatient's department or specialist unit
An international psychiatric research institute and a forensic detention facility

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): The RPMN is also described as working with specific patient populations. Which group is explicitly mentioned as being served by the RPMN?
options: Children with developmental or learning disabilities; adults and older people with various mental health conditions; those in general patient care settings coping with stresses associated with their general conditions
Children with psychiatric disorders only; adults with substance abuse problems exclusively; elderly patients with dementia across all inpatient settings
Children under 5 years with behavioural disorders; adults with treatment-resistant schizophrenia; elderly patients in long-term care facilities across community settings
School-aged children with learning disabilities only; working-age adults with anxiety disorders; elderly patients with Alzheimer's disease in specialised memory care units

stem: The Canadian Psychiatric Association defines psychiatrists using a specific phrase. Which definition is MOST precise and complete?
options: Physicians who enhance the person's quality of life by providing psychiatric assessment, treatment and rehabilitation care to people with psychiatric disorders, in order to prevent, reduce and eliminate the symptoms and subsequent disabilities resulting from mental illness or disorder

Physicians who enhance the patient's quality of care by providing diagnostic assessment, pharmacological treatment, and long-term follow-up for people with psychiatric disorders, in order to prevent relapse and promote social rehabilitation

Physicians who improve the person's quality of functioning by providing comprehensive biopsychosocial assessment, medication management, and psychotherapy to people with psychiatric disorders, in order to achieve symptom remission and occupational recovery

Physicians who maximise the person's quality of recovery by providing psychiatric evaluation, diagnosis, and evidence-based treatment for people with severe mental illness, in order to reduce hospitalisation rates and promote community reintegration

stem: Psychiatrists are described as one of only a few mental health professionals who can conduct physical examinations. Which additional unique capabilities of psychiatrists are mentioned in the notes?
options: Order and interpret laboratory tests and EEGs, order brain imaging studies such as CT or CAT, MRI, and PET scanning, and evaluate the patient for medical problems that may be the cause of the mental illness

Order and interpret laboratory tests, conduct electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), prescribe all classes of medication including controlled substances, and admit patients involuntarily to psychiatric facilities
Order laboratory tests and CT scans, provide emergency psychiatric services, prescribe psychotropic medications, and supervise other mental health professionals in the multidisciplinary team
Conduct physical examinations, order laboratory tests, perform surgical procedures on the brain, and interpret PET scans to diagnose treatment-resistant psychiatric disorders

stem: CASE STUDY: A patient asks a mental health nurse: 'What is the difference between a psychiatrist and a clinical psychologist? Both treat mental illness.' The MOST accurate and complete response, based on the notes, is:
options: Psychiatrists are physicians licensed to prescribe medications, conduct physical examinations, and order laboratory and brain imaging studies; clinical psychologists aim to reduce psychological distress and promote psychological well-being through assessment and therapy — using psychological tests, interviews, and direct observation — without prescribing medications

Psychiatrists and clinical psychologists have identical clinical roles; the only difference is that psychiatrists work in hospital settings while clinical psychologists work exclusively in community and school settings

Psychiatrists focus exclusively on biological treatment using medications; clinical psychologists focus exclusively on psychotherapy and cannot conduct any form of clinical assessment in medical settings

Psychiatrists are trained as physicians who provide long-term follow-up; clinical psychologists are trained to conduct short-term therapy only and must refer all complex cases back to the psychiatrist for ongoing management

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): The nurse also explains the role of the psychiatric social worker. Which statement MOST accurately describes the psychiatric social worker's role as stated in the notes?
options: Psychiatric social workers assess, treat, develop treatment plans, and provide case management and/or rights advocacy to individuals with mental health problems; they can work independently or within clinics/service agencies; they help people cope with mental health issues, provide counselling, help obtain financial and medical resources, and investigate housing and job placement options

Psychiatric social workers provide psychotherapy and medication management to individuals with mental health problems; they work exclusively within psychiatric hospitals and are responsible for coordinating inpatient and outpatient treatment planning across all disciplines

Psychiatric social workers conduct psychological assessments, provide cognitive behavioural therapy, and coordinate community mental health services; they require a doctoral degree and are licensed to prescribe psychotropic medications in most jurisdictions

Psychiatric social workers focus exclusively on case management and housing placement for homeless individuals with mental illness; they are not trained to provide therapy or counselling and must refer therapeutic needs to clinical psychologists

stem: The notes state that a healthcare environment is therapeutic when it does ALL of the following. Which option MOST completely and accurately lists these criteria?
options: Supports clinical excellence in treatment of the physical body; supports the psycho-social and spiritual needs of the patient, family, and staff; and produces measurable positive effects on patients' clinical outcomes and staff effectiveness

Supports clinical excellence in treatment of the physical body; supports the psycho-social needs of the patient and family only; and produces measurable positive effects on patients' clinical outcomes only

Supports clinical excellence in treatment of the physical body; supports the psychological needs of the patient; provides safe medication administration; and produces measurable positive effects on discharge rates

Supports clinical excellence in treatment of the physical body; supports the spiritual needs of the patient and staff; enables social support; and gives a sense of control to all patients in the facility

stem: Four key factors that can measurably improve patient outcomes when applied in healthcare environment design are identified in the notes. Which option MOST correctly and completely lists all four?
options: Reduce or eliminate environmental stressors; provide positive distractions; enable social support; give a sense of control
Reduce or eliminate environmental stressors; provide positive distractions; enable social support; promote patient recovery through activity
Reduce or eliminate environmental stressors; enable social support; give a sense of control; provide structured therapeutic programming
Eliminate environmental noise; provide positive distractions; enable family contact; give a sense of control over treatment decisions

stem: CASE STUDY: A new nurse asks her preceptor: 'What specific roles does the psychiatric mental health nurse perform according to Kiddick (2010)?' The preceptor lists the roles. Which combination of roles is explicitly mentioned in the notes?
options: Caring for patients with acute mental distress; assessing and talking to patients about problems; building trust relationships; ensuring correct medication administration and monitoring; responding to distressed patients non-threateningly; applying de-escalation techniques; participating in group and one-to-one therapy; preparing care plans; producing care plans and risk assessments; working with families and carers; promoting a recovery-based approach

Caring for patients with acute mental distress; administering electroconvulsive therapy; building therapeutic alliances; prescribing psychotropic medications; responding to distressed patients; applying restraint techniques; facilitating group therapy; preparing care plans; producing discharge summaries; educating families; promoting a rehabilitation-based approach

Caring for patients with acute and chronic mental illness; conducting psychological assessments; building trust relationships; administering medications; responding to violent patients; applying behavioural modification techniques; leading group therapy; preparing nursing notes; producing incident reports; educating carers; promoting a compliance-based approach

Caring for patients experiencing acute mental distress; assessing patients' behaviour and psychological needs; identifying when patients are at risk of harming themselves; visiting patients in their home; ensuring legal requirements are observed; liaising with community treatment teams; and coordinating care of patients in the community

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q11): The preceptor makes an important distinction between the RPMN's hospital roles and community roles. Which role is listed as specific to COMMUNITY practice of the RPMN?
options: Visiting patients in their home to monitor progress; assessing patients' behaviour and psychological needs; identifying whether and when patients are at risk of harming themselves or others
Caring for patients experiencing acute mental distress; building relationships with patients to encourage trust; applying de-escalation techniques to help people manage emotions and behaviour
Participating in group and/or one-to-one therapy sessions; preparing and maintaining patient records; ensuring correct administration of medications
Responding to distressed patients in a non-threatening manner; preparing and participating in therapy sessions; working with patients' families and carers

stem: The characteristics of the physical environment in a healthcare facility affect specific outcomes. Which combination is MOST precisely listed in the notes?
options: Patient outcomes, patient satisfaction, patient safety, staff efficiency, staff satisfaction, and organisational outcomes
Patient outcomes, patient safety, staff efficiency, staff satisfaction, family satisfaction, and organisational outcomes
Patient outcomes, patient satisfaction, patient safety, staff morale, staff satisfaction, and financial outcomes
Patient outcomes, patient experience, patient safety, staff efficiency, staff wellbeing, and institutional accreditation outcomes

stem: CASE STUDY: A psychiatric hospital administrator asks a nurse consultant to justify redesigning the ward environment. The nurse cites a key statement from the notes. Which statement is she MOST likely to use?
options: 'No environment is neutral' — the physical environment directly affects patient outcomes, patient satisfaction, patient safety, staff efficiency, staff satisfaction, and organisational outcomes; effects can be positive or negative

'All environments are therapeutic' — when healthcare facilities are designed with patient safety and staff efficiency in mind, the environment automatically supports patient recovery without additional therapeutic input

'The environment is secondary to care quality' — while environmental factors contribute to wellbeing, the skill of the nursing staff is the primary determinant of patient outcomes in psychiatric settings

'Environment shapes behaviour' — the large, complex environment of a typical hospital suppresses the immune system, making environmental redesign essential for physical rather than psychological recovery

stem: The notes state that nursing is a service initiated for a specific purpose. What purpose is MOST precisely stated?
options: Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of effecting change — a nurse must be able to identify needed changes and bring about change in a purposeful, thoughtful manner
Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of promoting healing — a nurse must be able to identify areas requiring clinical intervention and implement evidence-based treatments
Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of supporting recovery — a nurse must be able to identify functional deficits and implement rehabilitation strategies
Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of ensuring safety — a nurse must be able to identify risk factors and implement protective interventions

stem: The notes state that change is therapeutic under a specific condition. Which statement MOST precisely captures this?
options: Change is therapeutic when it alleviates the patient's distress
Change is therapeutic when it is evidence-based and implemented within a structured clinical framework
Change is therapeutic when it is initiated by the patient rather than imposed by the nurse
Change is therapeutic when it occurs gradually over time as part of a recovery-based approach to care

stem: CASE STUDY: A behaviour analyst is introduced to the psychiatric team. A student nurse asks what uniquely qualifies them. Which description is MOST accurate based on the notes?
options: Behaviour analysts are licensed to provide services for clients with substance abuse, developmental disabilities, and mental illness; they draw on applied behaviour analysis, behaviour therapy, and behaviourism; they have at least a master's in behaviour analysis or a related discipline and complete at least five core courses in applied behaviour analysis

Behaviour analysts are licensed to provide psychotherapy and pharmacological consultation for clients with developmental disabilities and substance abuse; they hold a minimum of a Bachelor's degree in psychology and complete specialised training in cognitive-behavioural therapy techniques

Behaviour analysts are licensed to diagnose and treat mental illness and developmental disabilities; they draw on psychoanalytic theory and behaviourism; they must hold a doctoral degree and complete supervised clinical hours in a licensed psychiatric facility

Behaviour analysts are licensed to provide case management and vocational rehabilitation for clients with mental illness and developmental disabilities; they apply principles of social learning theory; they hold at least a master's degree in rehabilitation counselling

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q17): A student asks how a counselling psychologist differs from a clinical psychologist. Which statement MOST accurately captures the distinction from the notes?
options: Clinical psychologists tend to conduct more projective and objective assessment and are more likely to work in hospitals or private practice; counselling psychologists conduct more vocational assessment, less projective assessment, and are more likely to work in public service or university clinics — despite these differences there is considerable overlap between the two fields

Clinical psychologists focus exclusively on diagnosis and treatment of severe mental illness; counselling psychologists focus exclusively on adjustment problems and life changes and cannot treat any diagnosable psychiatric disorder

Clinical psychologists and counselling psychologists are interchangeable in practice; the only difference is that counselling psychologists complete fewer supervised clinical hours and are licensed only for private practice settings

Clinical psychologists work only in hospitals and clinics; counselling psychologists work only in schools and universities — their client populations do not overlap and their therapeutic techniques are fundamentally different

stem: To intervene effectively as a therapeutic agent, the nurse must possess specific self-knowledge. Which statement from the notes MOST precisely captures what is required?
options: It is essential to know how one's behaviour affects others; change is therapeutic when it alleviates the patient's distress; to use oneself effectively as a change agent requires intervening effectively in the nurse-patient situation

It is essential to know how one's clinical decisions affect patient outcomes; therapeutic change requires evidence-based interventions; effective nursing requires ongoing professional development and self-reflection

It is essential to know how one's emotions affect clinical judgement; therapeutic use of self requires suppressing personal feelings to maintain professional boundaries in all nurse-patient interactions

It is essential to know how one's communication style affects patient disclosure; therapeutic use of self requires developing culturally sensitive approaches to establish trust across diverse patient populations

stem: CASE STUDY: A mental health nurse is asked to justify why stress from hospitalisation can impede patient recovery. She cites a specific mechanism from the notes. Which explanation is MOST accurate?
options: Stress can cause a person's immune system to be suppressed and can dampen a person's emotional and spiritual resources, impeding recovery and healing

Stress from hospitalisation directly activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, causing cortisol elevation that suppresses immune function and disrupts sleep architecture, impeding physical recovery

Stress in hospital settings increases sympathetic nervous system activity, causing elevated heart rate and blood pressure that interfere with the parasympathetic rest-and-digest response needed for healing

Stress from the large, complex hospital environment creates cognitive overload that reduces medication adherence and therapeutic engagement, indirectly impeding recovery through behavioural mechanisms

stem: Psychiatric social workers have educational requirements for licensing. Which requirement is MOST accurately stated in the notes?
options: State licensing boards and national certification boards require clinical social workers to have a master's or doctoral degree (MSW or DSW/PhD) from a university; the doctorate requires submission of a major original contribution to the field in order to be awarded the degree

State licensing boards require clinical social workers to have a minimum of a Bachelor's degree in social work (BSW) with specialised coursework in mental health; doctoral preparation is only required for academic or research positions

National certification boards require clinical social workers to have a master's degree (MSW) only; doctoral preparation (DSW/PhD) is optional and provides no additional clinical privileges beyond the MSW level

Licensing boards require clinical social workers to have either a master's in social work (MSW) or a doctorate in psychology (PhD) but not both; submission of original research is only required for the doctoral route

stem: CASE STUDY: A hospital administrator proposes that a psychiatrist should take on the therapeutic roles of the RPMN to save costs. A senior nurse correctly objects. Which argument MOST specifically distinguishes the RPMN's distinct role from the psychiatrist's?
options: The RPMN builds relationships with patients to encourage trust; listens to and interprets their needs and concerns; applies de-escalation techniques; prepares care plans and risk assessments; promotes recovery-based care; and delivers patient-centred multidisciplinary care — functions central to nursing that are distinct from the physician's diagnostic and prescribing role

The RPMN prescribes medications and conducts physical examinations — roles that overlap with the psychiatrist — but additionally provides psychotherapy and counselling, which are outside the psychiatrist's scope of practice

The RPMN is more qualified than the psychiatrist in mental health care because nursing preparation includes both biological and psychosocial training, while psychiatric preparation focuses narrowly on biological treatment

The RPMN's role is identical to the psychiatrist's in community settings; the distinction only applies in inpatient settings where role boundaries are defined by hospital policy rather than professional scope

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q22): The administrator then asks about the multidisciplinary team. Which professionals does the RPMN liaise with, according to Kiddick (2010)?
options: Psychiatrists, psychologists, occupational therapists, GPs, social workers, and other health professionals
Psychiatrists, pharmacists, occupational therapists, social workers, and physiotherapists
Psychiatrists, psychologists, nutritionists, social workers, and hospital chaplains
Psychiatrists, psychologists, clinical educators, GPs, and community health officers

stem: The UK College of Psychiatrists defines psychiatrists using two specific characteristics. Which definition is MOST precisely stated?
options: Psychiatrists are informed and uniquely skilled in the integration of medicine, psychiatry, neuroscience, and the psychosocial sciences
Psychiatrists are informed and uniquely skilled in the integration of medicine, psychiatry, psychopharmacology, and the social sciences
Psychiatrists are informed and uniquely skilled in the integration of medicine, neuroscience, psychology, and the psychosocial sciences
Psychiatrists are informed and uniquely skilled in the integration of psychiatry, neuroscience, rehabilitation science, and the psychosocial sciences

stem: CASE STUDY: A counselling psychology graduate student asks a psychiatric nurse why counselling psychologists tend to work in different settings than clinical psychologists. The MOST accurate answer from the notes is:
options: Counselling psychologists are more likely to work in public service or university clinics rather than hospitals or private practice because they tend to conduct more vocational assessment and less projective or objective assessment compared to clinical psychologists

Counselling psychologists work in university clinics exclusively because they are trained primarily as researchers and educators, not as clinicians, and therefore cannot practice in hospital or private clinical settings

Counselling psychologists work in public service settings because licensing boards require all counselling psychology graduates to complete a mandatory two-year public service commitment before private practice is permitted

Counselling psychologists prefer university and school settings because their training is rooted in developmental psychology, making them most effective with student populations rather than clinical psychiatric populations

stem: The notes describe a specific challenge psychiatrists have historically faced regarding prescription authority. Which statement MOST precisely reflects the current situation?
options: Historically, psychiatrists have been the only mental health professional with the power to prescribe medication to treat specific types of mental illness; currently, Physician Assistants and advanced practice psychiatric nurses may also prescribe medications, including psychiatric medications

Historically, psychiatrists were the only mental health professionals who could prescribe medications; currently, clinical psychologists in all states have obtained prescription privileges and can prescribe any psychiatric medication

Historically, psychiatrists shared prescribing authority with clinical psychologists; currently, only psychiatrists retain prescribing authority as legislative changes have rescinded prescribing rights from all non-physician mental health professionals

Historically, prescription authority was shared between psychiatrists and general practitioners; currently, advanced practice psychiatric nurses have completely replaced psychiatrists as the primary prescribers of psychotropic medications

stem: CASE STUDY: A student nurse is asked to explain what 'therapeutic use of self' involves in conscious nursing intervention. She lists five elements. Which list is MOST accurate based on the notes?
options: Conscious use of one's personality, background, life skills, and knowledge to develop a connection to a person with mental illness with a view to effecting change in the patient
Conscious use of one's personality, clinical knowledge, communication skills, and empathy to establish a therapeutic relationship with a view to promoting symptom reduction in the patient
Conscious use of one's professional training, personal values, communication techniques, and therapeutic boundaries to facilitate the patient's insight into their maladaptive behaviour
Conscious use of one's nursing expertise, cultural competence, life experience, and interpersonal skills to support the patient's recovery and reintegration into their community

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q27): The student is then asked what specific personal qualities the nurse must develop for effective therapeutic use of self. Which pair is MOST precisely stated in the notes?
options: Self-insight and self-understanding
Self-awareness and self-disclosure
Self-reflection and self-regulation
Self-monitoring and self-efficacy

stem: Patients in a healthcare facility experience specific fears. Which statement from the notes MOST accurately identifies these?
options: Patients are often fearful and uncertain about their health, their safety, and their isolation from normal social relationships
Patients are often fearful and uncertain about their diagnoses, their prognosis, and their loss of independence from daily activities
Patients are often fearful and uncertain about their treatment procedures, their medication side effects, and their loss of privacy in institutional settings
Patients are often fearful and uncertain about their financial obligations, their family burden, and their loss of social identity following psychiatric admission

stem: Clinical psychology is described as a rapidly developing field. What specific additional contribution is mentioned that demonstrates this development?
options: Clinical psychology is rapidly developing and adding to the evidence base through research; some clinical psychologists work as trainers, teachers, and researchers in universities

Clinical psychology is rapidly developing through the integration of neuroscience and pharmacology; some clinical psychologists now collaborate directly with psychiatrists to manage medication decisions
Clinical psychology is rapidly developing through digital therapeutics and teletherapy; some clinical psychologists work as app developers and technology consultants for mental health platforms

Clinical psychology is rapidly developing through legislation expanding prescription authority; some clinical psychologists have lobbied successfully for independent prescribing rights in several jurisdictions

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is orienting a new staff member and explains the therapeutic environment concept. Which statement from the notes is the MOST critical foundational principle she must convey?
options: 'No environment is neutral' — the physical environment affects patient outcomes, patient satisfaction, patient safety, staff efficiency, staff satisfaction, and organisational outcomes, with effects being positive or negative
'All environments can become therapeutic' — with adequate staffing ratios and evidence-based protocols, any clinical environment can achieve therapeutic status regardless of its physical design
'The therapeutic environment is staff-dependent' — the most critical determinant of therapeutic outcomes is the quality of nursing care delivered, not the physical design of the environment

'Environment is one of many factors' — while the physical environment plays a supporting role, it is the therapeutic relationship between nurse and patient that primarily determines clinical outcomes in psychiatric settings

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q31): The new staff member asks: 'Why does the large, complex hospital environment specifically affect psychiatric patients?' The MOST accurate answer from the notes is:
options: The large, complex environment of a typical hospital further contributes to the stressful situation patients already face from fears about their health, safety, and isolation from normal social relationships; stress can suppress the immune system and dampen emotional and spiritual resources, impeding recovery

The large, complex hospital environment disorients psychiatric patients because they have reduced cognitive capacity to navigate complex spatial environments, leading to increased anxiety and behavioural disturbance

The large, complex hospital environment exposes psychiatric patients to excessive sensory stimulation that triggers psychotic episodes in vulnerable individuals, making environmental simplification a primary treatment priority

The large, complex hospital environment creates enforced social contact between patients with different diagnoses, increasing cross-contamination of maladaptive coping strategies and reducing individual therapeutic progress

stem: The British Psychological Society (2011) defined clinical psychology with a specific aim. What is the MOST precise statement of this definition?
options: Clinical psychology aims to reduce psychological distress and to enhance and promote psychological well-being; a wide range of psychological difficulties are dealt with, including anxiety, depression, relationship problems, learning disabilities, child and family problems, and serious mental illness

Clinical psychology aims to diagnose and treat psychological disorders and to enhance adaptive functioning; psychological difficulties treated include anxiety, depression, personality disorders, psychotic disorders, and substance abuse problems

Clinical psychology aims to reduce emotional suffering and to promote mental health literacy; psychological difficulties addressed include anxiety, depression, trauma, grief and loss, life transitions, and serious mental illness

Clinical psychology aims to reduce psychological distress and to promote physical health outcomes; a wide range of psychological difficulties are addressed including anxiety, depression, somatic disorders, chronic pain, and serious mental illness

stem: CASE STUDY: A psychiatric nurse is challenged by a new intern who says: 'The nurse's job is to control symptoms, not to effect change.' The nurse responds using the concept from the notes. Which response is MOST grounded in the text?
options: Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of effecting change; a nurse must identify needed changes and bring about change in a purposeful, thoughtful manner; to use oneself effectively as a change agent means intervening effectively in the nurse-patient situation, and change is therapeutic when it alleviates the patient's distress

Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of promoting recovery; the nurse's primary role is to support the patient's natural healing process through evidence-based interventions rather than actively directing therapeutic change

Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of ensuring safety; the nurse's primary function is risk management and prevention of harm, from which therapeutic change naturally follows as a secondary benefit

Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of building therapeutic relationships; change occurs organically within the therapeutic alliance without the nurse needing to consciously structure or direct the change process

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q34): The intern then asks whether the nurse must always initiate therapeutic change actively. The nurse explains the role of self-use in the process. Which statement from the notes is MOST relevant?
options: Nursing involves conscious use of one's personality, background, life skills, and knowledge to develop a connection to a person with mental illness with a view to effecting change — the nurse consciously uses self as an instrument of change

Nursing involves passive observation of the patient's spontaneous change process, with the nurse providing a safe and supportive presence that allows change to emerge organically without direct therapeutic direction

Nursing involves applying structured evidence-based protocols that guide the patient through a predetermined change process, with the nurse's personal qualities serving only a secondary supportive function

Nursing involves collaborative goal-setting with the patient, with change defined and controlled by the patient's preferences and readiness, and the nurse's role limited to psychoeducation and resource provision

stem: Which mental health professional is described in the notes as working in prison settings to help patients deal with 'lingering issues'?
options: Psychiatric social worker
Behaviour analyst
Clinical psychologist
Counselling psychologist

stem: CASE STUDY: A family member of a psychiatric inpatient asks: 'Can the nurse visiting my relative at home actually prescribe medications if needed?' The MOST accurate answer based on the notes is:
options: Currently, Physician Assistants and advanced practice psychiatric nurses may prescribe medications including psychiatric medications — so an advanced practice psychiatric nurse could potentially prescribe; historically this was exclusively the psychiatrist's role

All registered psychiatric nurses are licensed to prescribe medications including psychiatric medications in community settings; prescription authority is not restricted to advanced practice level in community nursing
No nurse — regardless of qualification level — can prescribe psychiatric medications; this remains exclusively the psychiatrist's domain in all healthcare settings without exception

A psychiatric nurse can prescribe non-psychiatric medications such as analgesics and antibiotics in community settings, but psychiatric medication prescribing remains exclusively within the psychiatrist's scope

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q37): The family member asks what the community RPMN specifically does during a home visit. Which activities are MOST accurately listed as community-specific roles?
options: Visiting patients in their home to monitor progress; assessing patients' behaviour and psychological needs; identifying whether and when patients are at risk of harming themselves or others; and liaising with patients, relatives, and fellow professionals in the community treatment team

Visiting patients at home to administer medications; conducting psychiatric assessments; and prescribing adjustments to medications based on observed symptom changes in the patient's natural environment

Visiting patients at home to conduct psychotherapy sessions; monitoring medication adherence through pill counts; and documenting clinical observations for the multidisciplinary team at the next scheduled case review

Visiting patients at home to provide occupational therapy; assessing home safety risks; and coordinating with social services to arrange community support packages for patients and their family caregivers

stem: Which of the following MOST completely and accurately describes the work settings of clinical psychologists as stated in the notes?
options: Health and social care settings including hospitals, health centres, community mental health teams, child and adolescent mental health services, and social services; some also work in universities as trainers, teachers, and researchers

Health and social care settings including hospitals, health centres, community mental health teams, forensic services, and substance abuse treatment centres; some work as consultants to private corporations on employee mental health programmes

Health and social care settings exclusively; clinical psychologists are not permitted to work in academic settings or engage in research activities due to ethical restrictions on dual roles in clinical practice

Health and social care settings including hospitals, outpatient clinics, and rehabilitation centres; most clinical psychologists work in the private sector with only a minority employed within national health systems

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse manager is evaluating whether her ward qualifies as a 'therapeutic environment.' She reviews the criteria. Which statement MOST accurately reflects what is required for a healthcare environment to be therapeutic?
options: It must support clinical excellence in treatment of the physical body; support the psycho-social and spiritual needs of the patient, family, AND staff; and produce measurable positive effects on patients' clinical outcomes AND staff effectiveness — all three criteria must be met simultaneously

It must prioritise patient safety above all other considerations; clinical excellence in physical treatment is secondary to creating a safe environment, and psychosocial needs are addressed only when safety is fully ensured

It must implement evidence-based nursing interventions consistently; environmental design factors are secondary to clinical practice quality in determining whether a ward qualifies as therapeutically appropriate

It must achieve patient satisfaction scores above a threshold defined by national standards; staff satisfaction and organisational outcomes are important but are considered separately from therapeutic environment designation

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q40): The ward scores well on clinical excellence but the nurse manager discovers staff morale is very low. Based on the notes, how does this affect the ward's therapeutic status?
options: Staff satisfaction is explicitly listed as one of the characteristics affected by the physical environment that influence whether a healthcare environment is therapeutic; low staff satisfaction therefore compromises the ward's therapeutic status even if clinical excellence is maintained
Staff morale is a human resources concern rather than a clinical concern; low staff morale does not affect the ward's therapeutic status provided clinical protocols are consistently implemented
Staff satisfaction affects only organisational outcomes such as retention and absenteeism; it has no direct bearing on patient outcomes or the ward's designation as a therapeutic environment

Staff morale is relevant to therapeutic environment designation only if it is directly observable by patients; if low morale is not expressed behaviourally in patient interactions, it does not compromise the therapeutic status of the environment

stem: A counselling psychologist and a counselling psychotherapist differ in their educational preparation. Which statement MOST accurately describes this from the notes?
options: One may practice as a counselling psychologist with a PhD or EdD; as a counselling psychotherapist with a Master's degree; counselling psychology graduate programs are commonly housed in departments of education
One may practice as a counselling psychologist with a Master's degree only; as a counselling psychotherapist with a PhD or EdD; both programs are housed in departments of psychology or social science

One may practice as a counselling psychologist with a Bachelor's degree plus additional certification; as a counselling psychotherapist with a Master's degree; programs are housed in both psychology and education departments

One may practice as a counselling psychologist with a Master's or PhD; as a counselling psychotherapist with a Bachelor's degree plus supervised clinical hours; both require the same national licensing examination

stem: CASE STUDY: A mental health nurse asks how a behaviour analyst qualifies to practice. Which specific credentialing pathway is mentioned in the notes?
options: Passing the examination offered by the behaviour analysis certification board OR the examination in clinical behaviour therapy by the World Association for Behaviour Analysis
Completing a doctoral program in applied behaviour analysis and submitting an original dissertation approved by the Association for Behaviour Analysis International
Obtaining a master's degree in behaviour analysis and completing 2000 supervised clinical hours in a licensed psychiatric facility
Passing a national licensing examination administered by the state psychology board and completing continuing education in applied behaviour analysis

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q43): The nurse asks where the model licensing act for behaviour analysts can be found. The MOST accurate answer from the notes is:
options: At the Association for Behaviour Analysis International's website
At the World Association for Behaviour Analysis's official registry
At the state psychology licensing board's online database
At the National Alliance for Mental Illness (NAMI) professional resources portal

stem: According to Wairangi (2011), which organisational skill is specifically listed as a personal requirement for mental health nurses?
options: Organisational and decision-making skills
Organisational and time-management skills
Organisational and prioritisation skills
Organisational and delegation skills

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is asked whether she needs to understand the Mental Health Act as a psychiatric nurse. Which statement MOST accurately reflects the notes?
options: An understanding of the Mental Health Act and other relevant legislation is explicitly listed by Wairangi (2011) as a personal requirement for mental health nurses

Understanding the Mental Health Act is important but is categorised as an administrative skill rather than a clinical skill; it is therefore not included in Wairangi's personal requirements for mental health nurses
Understanding the Mental Health Act is the responsibility of the ward manager and hospital legal team; bedside nurses are not expected to have personal knowledge of mental health legislation

Understanding the Mental Health Act is beneficial but optional for mental health nurses; the most critical personal requirements according to Wairangi are clinical nursing skills and knowledge of psychiatric disorders

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q46): The nurse is then asked which communication skills are specifically listed by Wairangi (2011). Which answer is MOST precise?
options: Oral and written communication skills including good listening skills, and good listening skills are listed separately as a standalone requirement
Oral communication skills only, with good listening skills embedded within this category as a non-separate requirement
Written communication skills only, since oral communication is considered an innate ability not requiring formal development for nursing practice
Oral, written, and non-verbal communication skills, with active listening, therapeutic silence, and motivational interviewing specifically highlighted

stem: Psychiatric social workers may investigate specific practical areas when working with patients. Which areas are mentioned in the notes?
options: Housing and job placement options for recovering patients
Housing options and educational retraining programmes for recovering patients
Job placement and financial management counselling for recovering patients
Housing, legal advocacy, and vocational rehabilitation for recovering patients

stem: CASE STUDY: A newly qualified RPMN asks what her specific role is in 'de-escalation.' Which statement from the notes MOST accurately captures this?
options: Applying 'de-escalation' techniques to help people manage their emotions and behaviour
Applying 'de-escalation' techniques to prevent violence and reduce the need for physical restraint in acute inpatient settings
Applying 'de-escalation' techniques to reduce medication requirements by substituting psychosocial intervention for pharmacological sedation
Applying 'de-escalation' techniques to help patients develop insight into the triggers of their emotional dysregulation

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q49): The new RPMN asks how her role in therapy sessions is described in the notes. Which is the MOST accurate description?
options: Preparing and participating in group and/or one-to-one therapy sessions, both individually and with other health professionals
Leading group therapy sessions independently and conducting one-to-one cognitive behavioural therapy sessions without supervision
Observing group therapy sessions conducted by the clinical psychologist and providing post-session emotional support to individual patients
Facilitating group therapy sessions focused exclusively on medication psychoeducation and administering one-to-one motivational interviewing sessions

stem: According to Wairangi (2011), mental health nurses require oral and written communication skills including good listening skills, and _______________ and decision-making skills.

CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'The ability to use one's personality consciously and in full awareness in an attempt to establish relatedness and to structure nursing intervention is defined as the _______________ use of self.'

The notes state that a healthcare environment is designed not only to support state-of-the-art medicine, technology, patient safety, and quality patient care, but also to embrace the patient, family, and caregivers in a psycho-socially supportive _______________ environment.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'The notes state that no environment is _______________. Effects of the physical environment can be positive or negative.' Only one word completes this sentence precisely.

The Canadian Psychiatric Association defines psychiatrists as physicians who enhance the person's quality of life by providing psychiatric assessment, treatment, and _______________ care to people with psychiatric disorders.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The UK College of Psychiatrists says psychiatrists are informed and uniquely skilled in the integration of medicine, psychiatry, _______________, and the psychosocial sciences.' One discipline is missing.

Nursing is a service initiated for the purpose of _______________ change (Elder et al., 2009); a nurse must be able to identify needed changes and bring about change in a purposeful, thoughtful manner.
CASE STUDY: During a ward orientation, a nurse states: 'Change is therapeutic when it _______________ the patient's distress.' Students must supply the precise verb used in the notes.
The British Psychological Society (2011) states that clinical psychology aims to reduce psychological _______________ and to enhance and promote psychological well-being.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'According to the notes, in maintaining a therapeutic milieu, the nurse _______________ the environment to encourage recovery of the patient.' Only one verb is correct.
The therapeutic use of self requires self-insight, self-understanding, and understanding of the _______________ of human behaviour.

CASE STUDY: A nurse exam reads: 'According to Kiddick (2010), the psychiatric mental health nurse liaises with psychiatrists, psychologists, occupational therapists, GPs, social workers and other health professionals to plan and deliver care using a _______________ patient-centred approach.'
Psychiatric social workers are employed in many different settings, including inpatient psychiatric hospitals, outpatient mental health centres, prisons, and _______________ social service offices.

CASE STUDY: An exam vignette reads: 'The RPMN responds to distressed patients in a non-threatening manner and attempts to understand the _______________ of distress.' Students must supply the precise noun used in the notes.
Behaviour analysts have at least a master's degree in behaviour analysis or in a mental health related discipline AND at least _______________ core courses in applied behaviour analysis.

CASE STUDY: A student is asked: 'The notes list four key factors that can measurably improve patient outcomes in healthcare environment design. Name the third factor.' The correct answer is: enable _______________ _______________.
The doctorate in social work requires submission of a _______________ original contribution to the field in order to be awarded the degree.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'The specific personal requirements according to Wairangi (2011) include excellent nursing skills AND knowledge of different nursing _______________ and theories.' Students must supply the exact word used.
Compared with clinical psychology, counselling psychology graduate programs are more commonly housed in departments of _______________.
CASE STUDY: A student nurse is asked: 'The RPMN works with patients to come to terms with illness in order to _______________ their life potential.' One precise verb from the notes must be supplied.
